attributes.stem:
A 91-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with a three-day history of severe, sharp and constant back pain. He has a medical history of metastatic castrate-resistant prostate cancer. He has experienced double incontinence for the past week and decreased mobility. He is now unable to walk, whereas his baseline mobility required a four-wheeled walker. He does not take any regular medications. He lives at home with his wife and has a supportive daughter.

On examination, he appears unwell. His pulse rate is 90/min, blood pressure 130/80 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 97% on room air, and temperature 37.0°C. There is tenderness on palpation of the thoracic spine. Neurological examination reveals decreased sensation and power in both lower limbs.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate investigation?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with symptoms and signs highly suspicious for malignant spinal cord compression, which is a palliative care emergency. The history of metastatic prostate cancer, new onset of severe back pain, progressive neurological deficits (double incontinence and decreased mobility), and examination findings of spinal tenderness and lower limb neurological deficits are all consistent with this diagnosis.

MRI spine is the most appropriate investigation in this case. It is the gold standard for diagnosing malignant spinal cord compression, with a high sensitivity of 93% and specificity of 97%. MRI of the whole spine should be performed to ensure that all levels of spinal cord compression are identified, even if asymptomatic. Malignant spinal cord compressions are most likely to occur at the thoracic level.

CT scans are less sensitive than MRI scans when detecting soft tissue metastases. They are typically performed when there are contraindications to MRI or when MRI is not available. While CT can show bony involvement, it may miss early cord compression.

CT myelogram involves injecting contrast into the spinal canal before performing a CT scan. This allows for greater detail of the vertebrae and appreciation of the degree of spinal canal stenosis. However, it still lacks the soft tissue detail that MRI provides, which is crucial for assessing malignant spinal cord compression. Additionally, it is an invasive procedure with potential complications.

X-ray spine is a poor choice of imaging modality for suspected spinal cord compression. While it may show vertebral abnormalities or degeneration, it would not demonstrate nerve or cord damage. X-rays lack the sensitivity to detect early spinal cord compression.

Ultrasound spine can be used to assess spinal canal stenosis or cord compression in certain situations, such as in neonates. However, it lacks the sensitivity of MRI and, for practical reasons, it would be difficult to use ultrasound to image the whole spine in an adult patient. It is not a standard investigation for suspected malignant spinal cord compression.

In summary, given the high suspicion for malignant spinal cord compression in this patient with metastatic prostate cancer, an urgent MRI of the whole spine is the most appropriate investigation. This will allow for accurate diagnosis and guide further management, which may include urgent radiotherapy, steroids, and neurosurgical intervention if appropriate.
learningPoints: Malignant spinal cord compression is a palliative care emergency.
MRI of the whole spine is the gold standard investigation for suspected malignant spinal cord compression, with high sensitivity and specificity.
references: Metastatic spinal cord compression in adults: risk assessment, diagnosis, and management, Clinical Guideline. In: National Institute for Health and Clinical Excellence (NICE) Guidelines. 2014; updated 2023. Available at: https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/qs56
